What is Svabhāva — and what does the Śāstra do to the Seeker?

# What is Svabhāva — and what does the Śāstra do to the Seeker?

### Reflections on Bhagavad Gītā 17.2 · Śaṅkarabhāṣya & Gūḍhārthadīpikā

[](https://substack.com/@jamvasu)

![Srinivas Jammalamadaka's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!r4yD!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8e86879b-57b7-418c-8baf-81252d34c6c4_96x96.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Hcr8!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8d90d8cf-7267-4639-913a-b411a82cf6ab_1254x1254.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

When Arjuna asks Kṛṣṇa about the nature of śraddhā — whether it is sāttvika, rājasika, or tāmasika — the reply opens with a deceptively simple word: *svabhāva*. What is this “own nature” that determines the quality of a person’s śraddhā? Two of the tradition’s greatest commentators, Śaṅkarācārya and Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, give answers that are worth reading together.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Śaṅkara: svabhāva as the saṃskāra carried across births**

Thanks for reading! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

Subscribe

Śaṅkara’s definition is lapidary:

जन्मान्तरकृतः धर्मादिसंस्कारः मरणकाले अभिव्यक्तः स्वभावः

— Śaṅkarabhāṣya on BG 17.2

*Svabhāva is the saṃskāra of dharma and so on, produced across prior births, and made manifest at the time of death.*

Two things stand out here. First, svabhāva is not something innate in an abstract metaphysical sense — it is *made*, accumulated across lifetimes of action and intention. The word *kṛta* (”done, produced”) is deliberate. Second, it becomes visible — *abhivyakta* — at the moment of death. This is the moment when all the conditioning of a lifetime crystallises and the latent tendency that will propel the next birth rises to the surface. Śaṅkara is pointing to a very specific juncture: svabhāva is not just background temperament, it is the operative force that carries the trajectory of the ātman forward.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Madhusūdana: svabhāva, the śāstra, and the three kinds of śraddhā**

Madhusūdana Sarasvatī, writing in his *Gūḍhārthadīpikā*, builds directly on Śaṅkara’s foundation while adding a crucial distinction:

जन्मान्तरकृतो धर्माधर्मादिशुभाशुभसंस्कार इदानींतनजन्मारम्भकः स्वभावः। या त्वारब्धे जन्मनि शास्त्रसंस्कारमात्रजा विदुषां सा कारणैकरूपत्वादेकरूपा सात्त्विक्येव न राजसी तामसी।

— Gūḍhārthadīpikā on BG 17.2

He defines svabhāva as the accumulated saṃskāras of meritorious and demeritorious actions — dharma and adharma, the auspicious and inauspicious — from prior births, and identifies it as the force that *initiates the present birth* (*idānīntana-janma-ārambhakaḥ*). This is the engine of the jīva’s current embodiment.

But then Madhusūdana makes a striking exception: for the *viduṣāṃ* — the learned, those who have genuinely engaged with the śāstra — a different kind of śraddhā arises in this very birth. Śraddhā that is born solely from *śāstra-saṃskāra*, the purification wrought by scriptural study and understanding, is *necessarily sāttvika*. It is single in form (*ekarūpā*) because its cause — the śāstra itself — is of a single, uniform nature. It is not rājasika, not tāmasika. The śāstra, properly engaged, admits of only one outcome at the level of śraddhā.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**The decisive sentence: what happens without the śāstra**

Then comes the sentence that gives the whole commentary its edge:

यतः प्राग्भवीयवासनाख्यस्वभावस्याभिभावकं शास्त्रीयं विवेकविज्ञानमनादृतशास्त्राणां देहिनां नास्ति अतस्तेषां स्वभाववशात् त्रिधाभवन्तीं तां श्रद्धां

— Gūḍhārthadīpikā on BG 17.2

*Because for those embodied beings who have disregarded the śāstra, there is no śāstric viveka-vijñāna — the discriminative knowledge born of scripture — which could overpower the svabhāva consisting of vāsanās from prior births. Therefore, in those persons, under the force of svabhāva, śraddhā becomes threefold.*

This is perhaps the most philosophically precise statement in either commentary. Madhusūdana introduces a technical term: *abhibhāvaka* — that which *overpowers or subdues*. The svabhāva of prior-life vāsanās is not passive; it actively governs the quality of a person’s śraddhā. The only thing that can subdue it is the viveka — the discriminative wisdom — produced by the śāstra. Without that, svabhāva runs unchecked, and the threefold division of śraddhā (sāttvika, rājasika, tāmasika) reflects nothing but the accumulated weight of past karma.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**What this tells us about the function of the śāstra**

Reading both commentators together, a picture emerges that is simultaneously sobering and clarifying. Svabhāva is not fate — it is accumulated momentum. It can be redirected. But only one instrument is named as powerful enough to do so: the śāstra, when it is not merely studied but truly received — *ādṛta*, honoured, taken seriously. The word Madhusūdana uses for those who fail is *anādṛta-śāstrāṇām*: those who have not honoured the śāstra.

The śāstra, in this framing, is not a rulebook. It is a force of *abhibhāvaka* — it counteracts, it suppresses, it re-orients the deep grooves of prior conditioning. And the viveka it produces is not mere intellectual understanding; it is a form of knowing powerful enough to overwrite vāsanās that have been accumulating across lifetimes.

This is what it means to say that śraddhā in the sāttvika person is born of śāstra-saṃskāra alone. Their śraddhā does not carry the grain and texture of their karmic past — it has been remade by the encounter with the śāstra. For everyone else, śraddhā is simply a mirror of who they have been across births.

The question the commentaries leave with the seeker is quietly urgent: am I among those who have truly honoured the śāstra — or am I still, without knowing it, acting entirely under the force of svabhāva?
